params.description: Petworld HIV virion model consisting of 37.7 million atoms that carries two at the dimerization initiation sequence coupled RNA strands inside the capsid core particle. Authors: Kornel Ozvoldik, Elmar Krieger and the CellPack team.

params.customText: ⑤Enzymes Critical to HIV's Life Cycle
params.tooltip: Detailed view of the key enzymes: reverse transcriptase, integrase, and protease, showing their molecular structures and positions relative to the RNA.

HIV protease is essential for the maturation of HIV particles. The proteins in HIV are built as long polyproteins, which then must be cleaved into the proper functional pieces by HIV protease. Protease inhibitors are widely used as anti-HIV drugs, often in combination with drugs that block reverse transcriptase and integrase. PDB entry 1hpv

Integrase takes the DNA copy of the viral genome and inserts it into the infected cellular genome. In this way, HIV can lie dormant in cells for decades, making it incredibly difficult to fight. Anti-HIV drugs that block integrase have been developed. PDB entry 1hys

Reverse transcriptase builds a DNA copy of the viral RNA genome, which is then used to build new viruses. This structure captures the enzyme as it is building a DNA strand (red) from the viral RNA (yellow). It will then destroy the RNA and build a second DNA strand. Many of the drugs currently used to fight HIV infection block the action of reverse transcriptase. PDB entry 1hys

﻿The genome of HIV is composed of two identical strands of RNA, each about 9700 nucleotides long. The genome encodes the information needed to synthesize all of the viral proteins. Portions of the genome also form hairpin loops that interact with the regulatory proteins.
Zoom out showing everything
Nucleocapsid protein forms a stable complex with the viral RNA, protecting it. In this structure, a short piece of RNA (yellow) is bound to one copy of nucleocapsid (orange). PDB entry 1a1t.
A close-up on the RNA strands, showcasing association with nucleocapsid proteins.

params.customText: ③ The Capsid and Its Contents
params.tooltip: Illustration focusing on the conical capsid structure, cut away to show the interior. Display the RNA and enzymes within.
viral envelope

Envelope proteins gp120 and gp41 bind to receptors on the surface of cells that HIV infects, and then penetrate the surface to infect it with the viral RNA. The spikes formed by these proteins are highly decorated with carbohydrates, making them difficult to recognize by antibodies. PDB entry 4nco includes SU and the portion of TM that is outside the membrane.

Matrix protein forms a coat on the inner surface of the viral membrane. It plays a central role when new viruses bud from the surface of infected cells. This protein assembles into trimers, which then associate side-by-side on the membrane. PDB entry 1hiw.

Negative regulatory factor forces the infected cell to stop making several proteins that are important in cell defense. Nef is important in the progression of HIV infection to Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome (AIDS). PDB entries 1avv and 1qa5.

params.customText: ② The Viral Envelope and Surface Proteins
params.tooltip: Zoom in on the viral envelope. Display the gp120 and gp41 proteins in high detail, showing their structure and positioning on the envelope.

entries.name: Start
entries.description:
**Human Immunodeficiency Virus HIV**

HIV is composed of [two strands of RNA](iGenome), [15 types of viral proteins](igp160,Vpu,Capsid,MA,Nef,Tat,RT,IN,NC,Vpr,Vif,PR,P6,SP1,SP2), and a few proteins from the last host cell it infected, all surrounded by a [lipid bilayer membrane](imembrane_rho,membrane_tri). Together, these molecules allow the virus to infect cells of the immune system and force them to build new copies of the virus. Each molecule in the virus plays a role in this process, from the first steps of viral attachment to the final process of budding.
Since 1986, research on the structural biology of HIV has revealed the atomic details of these proteins. These structures are all publicly available in the [Protein Data Bank](https://www.rcsb.org/) archive. Using these data, researchers have designed new treatments for HIV infection, including effective drug regimens that halt the growth of the virus. The structures also provide new hope for development of a vaccine. 

[Model Source](http://download.yasara.org/petworld/hiv/index.html).

Next to [Envelope](#B).

Or go to :

- [Capsid](#C)
- [Genetic](#D)
- [Enzymes](#E)
- [Explore](#AA)

entries.name: Envelope
entries.description:
**The Viral Envelope**

A critical interface for HIV, composed of a lipid bilayer studded with [gp120 and gp41](igp160) proteins. These proteins are instrumental in recognizing and binding to host cells, initiating the infection process.

Next to [Capsid](#C)

entries.name: Capsid
entries.description:
**Inside the Capsid**

Housing the virus's genetic blueprint and essential enzymes. This conical structure encapsulates [HIV's RNA](iGenome) and [critical enzymes](iRT,IN), safeguarding them until they are ready to commandeer the host's machinery.

Next to [Genetic](#D)

entries.name: Genetic
entries.description:
**HIV's RNA**

The genetic core of the virus, consisting of two identical [RNA strands](iGenome). These strands hold the instructions for producing new virions and are intricately packed within the [capsid](iCapsid), poised for replication upon infection.

Next to [Enzymes](#E)

entries.name: Enzymes
entries.description:
**Enzymatic Trio**

[Reverse transcriptase](iRT) converts RNA into DNA, [Integrase](iIN) inserts this DNA into the host genome, and [Protease](iPR) cleaves newly formed viral proteins. Each plays a distinct yet crucial role in the HIV life cycle.

Back to [Start](#A)
